content.full_text:
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 26, 2007

Mr. Brian Oneto
P.O. Box 95
Drytown, CA 95699

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-181

Dear Mr. Oneto:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Given that you have two semi-trailers for storage on your ranch, do you have a
conflict of interest in participating in the review and possible amendment of Amador
County's sea/land storage container ordinance?

2. If you have a conflict of interest, can you sell or give away the semi-trailers,
removing them from your property, and then be free to vote?

CONCLUSIONS

1. It is substantially likely that your participation in the review or amendment of
the sea/land container ordinance would affect your personal finances by $250. Because
of this effect on your personal finances, you have a conflict of interest in the Board of
Supervisor's consideration of the sea/land container ordinance and may not participate.
You must recuse yourself as discussed in this letter.

2. If you sell the semi-trailers to an individual and remove them from your
property, you may participate in the matter unless the decision on the sea/land container
ordinance would affect the purchaser's "income, investments, or other tangible or

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-07-181
Page No. 2

intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more." Under this
standard, unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would affect the
individual's income from or the value of the trailer by $1,000, you may participate. In
addition, if you give the trailers away and remove them from your property, you may
participate in consideration of the sea/land storage container ordinance.

FACTS

You are a member of the Amador County Board of Supervisors, representing
District 5. In 2006, the Board approved Amador County Code 19.48.160, a copy of
which you provided to us, which is designed to regulate the use and placement of
sea/land and other similar storage containers. Because the ordinance was made
applicable to all such containers in the unincorporated County, it contains a provision that
delays enforcement until May 2008. In relevant part, Section 19.48.160(I) prohibits the
use of "railroad cars, cabooses, semi-trailers, or any other unit which slides off a chassis
or frame (including a body, box or unit which is removed from a chassis)" as storage
containers. Amador County is looking to possibly re-write its sea-land storage container
regulations.

You state that many public agencies have storage containers, i.e., semi-trailers,
sea/land containers, van bodies, etc. There are hundreds of these containers used for
storage throughout Amador County. Wal-Mart has at times 20 to 30 being used.
Containers are also used by the schools, the local water agency, California Department of
Forestry and Fire Protection, cities, non-profit groups, recycling centers, farmers,
ranchers, K-Mart, businesses, homeowners and many others.

You have two semi-trailers being used for storage on your ranch. The semi-
trailers were on your ranch being used for storage before the existing ordinance was
passed. They are complete trailers with axles, tires and all.

You ask whether you may vote on a proposed new sea/land storage container
ordinance or whether you have a conflict of interest. If a conflict of interest is present,
you ask if you can sell or give away the semi-trailers and then be free to vote?

You state that there are numerous storage containers in your district as well as the
county as a whole. It would be virtually impossible to obtain a count of them. On a drive
through just the main roads in the county, one spots storage containers everywhere. The
amount of containers used throughout this rural county is significant. Your district is
highly agricultural and depends on these containers. You believe District 5 needs
representation on this issue. You ask if you are on an equal footing basis with your
constituents and, as such, would you be able to vote under the public generally
exception?

File No. A-07-181
Page No. 3

In addition, you requested an opinion as to a possible conflict of interest of
another supervisor whose spouse and daughter are employed by a business that uses
semi-trailers and sea/land containers for storage. As we discussed by phone on
November 30, 2007, Commission staff can only provide advice to the requestor. We
cannot provide you advice concerning another individual's duties under the Act.
(Regulation 18329.)

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step analysis
for determining whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation
18700(b).)

Step 1. Are you a "public official"?

Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." As a member of the Amador County
Board of Supervisors, a local government agency, you are a public official subject to the
Act's conflict-of-interest rules. (Section 82041.) Therefore, you may not make,
participate in making, or otherwise use your position to influence any decisions that will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic interests.

Step 2. Will you be making, participating in making or influencing a
governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

The Amador County Board of Supervisors has begun reviewing the current
sea/land container ordinance, and is considering amending some of its provisions. As a

File No. A-07-181
Page No. 4

member of the Board of Supervisors, you would be making or participating in a
governmental decision in voting on or considering amendment of this ordinance.

Step 3. What are your economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from an official's economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section
87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, as follows:

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section
87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a).)

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
Regulation 18703.2.)

• An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $390 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family.
This is referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)
A governmental decision will have an effect on an official's personal finances
"if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or
decreasing." (Regulation 18703.5.)

Your economic interests that might be affected by the Board of Supervisors'
consideration of the sea/land storage container ordinance are your real property interest in
your ranch and your personal finances.

File No. A-07-181
Page No. 5

Step 4. Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved
in the governmental decision?

An economic interest which is directly involved in, and therefore, directly
affected by, a governmental decision creates a greater risk of a conflict-of-interest than
does an economic interest which is only indirectly involved in the decision. We next
determine whether your economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
decisions about the container ordinance.

Real property: Regulation 18704.2(b) states that real property is considered to be
indirectly involved in a decision if

"(1) The decision solely concerns the amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as changes in the uses
permitted, or development standards applicable, within a particular zoning
category) which is applicable to all other properties designated in that
category ...."

Here, the decisions before the Amador County Board of Supervisors concern use permits
for sea/land storage containers. Under Regulation 18704.2, your property is considered
to be indirectly involved in the decision.

Personal finances: Under Regulation 18704.5, "[a] public official or his or her
immediate family are deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision which
has any financial effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate
family." (Emphasis added.) Because decisions regarding the sea/land storage container
ordinance will affect your personal finances as discussed in Step 6 below, you are
considered directly involved in the decisions.

Step 5. What materiality standard applies?

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Different materiality standards apply depending on the type of
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.

Real property: The materiality standard for indirectly involved real property
applies to your economic interest in your ranch. Under Regulation 18705.2(b)(1), the
"financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material" unless specific
circumstances exist to rebut this presumption.

File No. A-07-181
Page No. 6

Personal Finances: For an official's personal finances, Regulation 18705.5 states that "[a] reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period."

Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decisions on your economic interests will meet the applicable materiality standard?

Once an official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

Real property: Because your ranch is only indirectly involved in the decisions about the container ordinance, the financial effect of the decisions is presumed not to be material. You have not described any specific circumstances regarding decisions about the container ordinance that would rebut this presumption and show that these decisions would have a material financial effect on your ranch. It is not reasonably foreseeable that modifications of the container ordinance will affect the property value of your ranch, the development or income producing potential of your ranch, or affect the use of your property or character of your neighborhood to any significant extent. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(A)-(C).) Therefore your real property interest in the ranch does not give rise to a conflict of interest for you.

Personal finances: In this case, it appears reasonably foreseeable that the Amador County Board of Supervisors's decision concerning the sea/land storage container ordinance would result in your personal expenses exceeding or decreasing by $250 in a 12-month period. If the ordinance is not amended, you will be required to sell, give away, or cease using the trailers for storage and will most likely incur at least $250 to relocate the items you have in the trailers and store them elsewhere. If the ordinance is amended to allow your use of the trailers to continue, you would be relieved of this expense. Thus, you have a conflict of interest and may not participate in or vote on the Board's consideration of the sea/land storage container ordinance.

Recusing yourself from the decision: As a member of the Board of Supervisors, you must follow certain procedures when disqualifying yourself from voting on a matter. (Section 87105 and 87200; Regulations 18702.1 and 18702.5.) If you have a conflict of interest in a decision to be considered at a noticed public meeting, then you must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, publicly identify on the record of the

File No. A-07-181
Page No. 7

meeting the type of economic interest involved in the decision (i.e., personal financial
effect); (2) recuse yourself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion
and/or vote on the item. (Regulation 18702.5, copy enclosed.) Regulation 18702.5(d)(1)
and (d)(3) discuss an official's recusal for conflicts with items on the consent calendar,
and speaking as a member of the general public as to an applicable personal interest.²

Steps 7. and 8. Do the "public generally" or the "legally required
participation" exceptions apply?

You asked whether the public generally exception might apply to your situation,
because storage containers are common in Amador County. Even if a material financial
effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, he or she still
may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental decision on the public
official's economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)).

To fit within the public generally exception as to the sea/land container ordinance,
you would have to obtain facts showing that the decision on the ordinance would also
affect the personal finances of (i) ten percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction
of your agency (i.e., Amador County) or your supervisoral district, or (ii) 5,000
individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction, in substantially the same manner as it
affects your personal finances. (Regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(A) and (b)(2).) In the facts
you submitted you observed that there are a lot of storage containers in use in Amador
County. However, you have not presented the facts necessary to fit within the public
generally exception. These facts may be difficult to gather, unless there is an existing
record of the numbers of individuals who hold permits for storage containers in Amador
County.

In addition, there is no indication that the narrow exception contained in
Regulation 18708 for situations where officials are legally required to participate in a
decision despite having a conflict of interest applies here. (Section 87103; Regulation
18708).

Selling or giving away the trailers: If you do have a conflict of interest, you asked
whether selling or giving away the trailers and removing them from your property would

2 Section 87105(a)(3) provides an exception for matters on consent calendars. When the matter in which the
public official has a financial interest is on the consent calendar, the public official must disclose the economic interests
giving rise to his or her conflict of interest (under Regulation 18702.5(b)(1) and (2)) and recuse himself from the vote.
However, the public official is not required to leave the room during the vote on a consent calendar. (Regulation
18702.5(d)(1).)

When a personal interest as identified in Regulation 18702.4(b) (copy enclosed) is affected, a public official
may speak as a member of the general public so long as he discloses the economic interests giving rise to his or her
conflict of interest (under Regulation 18702.5(b)(1) and (2)) and recuses himself from the vote. The official must then
leave the dais to speak from the same area as the members of the public. (Regulation 18702.5(d)(3).)

File No. A-07-181
Page No. 8


eliminate the conflict and permit you to vote. If you sell the semi-trailers, the individual
buys them will be a source of income to you. You will have an economic interest in the
purchaser and you may not make a governmental decision that will have a material
financial effect on that source of income. Here, the source of income is indirectly
involved in the container ordinance decisions (Regulation 18704.1(b).) If you sell the
semi-trailers to an individual and remove them from your property, you may participate
in the matter unless the decision on the sea/land container ordinance would affect the
purchaser's "income, investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by $1,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.3(b)(3).) Under this
standard, unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would affect the
individual's income from or the value of the trailers by $1,000, you may participate. In
addition, if you give the trailers away and remove them from your property, you may
participate in consideration of the sea/land storage container ordinance.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

HPW:jgl

Enclosures:   Can I Vote? Conflict of Interest Pamphlet
Regulations 18702.4, and 18702.5

you must follow certain procedures when disqualifying yourself from voting on a matter.

because storage containers are common in Amador County. Even if a material financial
effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, he or she still
may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental decision on the public
official's economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)).
To fit within the public generally exception as to the sea/land container ordinance,
you would have to obtain facts showing that the decision on the ordinance would also
affect the personal finances of (i) ten percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction
of your agency (i.e., Amador county) or your supervisoral distiict, or (ii) 5,00b
individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction, in substantially the same manner as it
affects your personal finances. (Regulation 18707.l(bx1)(A) and (b)(2).) In the facts
you submitted you observed that there are a lot of storage containers in use in Amador
County. However, you have not presented the facts necessary to fit within the public
generally exception. These facts may be difficult to gather, unless there is an eiisting
record of the numbers of individuals who hold permits for storage containers in Amador
County.
In addition, there is no indication that the narrow exception contained in
Regulation 18708 for situations where officials are legally required to participate in a
decision despite having a conflict of interest applies here. (Section 82i03; Rlgulation
18708).
Selline or giving away the trailers: If you do have a conflict of interest, you asked
whether selling or giving away the trailers and removing them from your prop"rty would
2 Section 87105(aX3) provides an exception for matters on consent calendars. when the matter in which the
public official has a financial interest is on the consent calendar, the public official must disclose the economic interests
giving rise to his or her conflict of interest (under Regulation t 8702.i(b)( I ) and (2)) and recuse himself flrom the vote.
However, the public official is not required to leave the room during the vote on a consent calendar. (Regulation
r8702.5(dX t).)
when a personal interest as identified in Regulation 18702.4(b) (copy enclosed) is affected. a public official
may speak as a member of-the general public so long as he discloses the economic interests giving rise ti his o, h.,
conflictofinterest(underRegulationl8702.5(b)(l)ancl(2))andrecuseshimselffromthevdte. iheotficialmusrthen
leave the dais to speak from the same area as the members of the public. The official may listen to tne funtic
discussionof themarterwiththemembersofthepublic. (Regularion 1g702.5(dx3).)
eliminate the conflict and permit you to vote. If you sell the semi-trailers, the individual
buys them will be a source of income to you. You will have an economic interest in the
purchaser and you may not make a govemmental decision that will have a material
financial effect on that source of income. Here, the source of income is indirectly
involved in the container ordinance decisions (Regulation 18704.1(b).) If you sell the
semi-trailers to an individual and remove them from your property, you may participate
in the matter unless the decision on the sea/land container ordinance would affect the
purchaser's "income, investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by $1,000 or more." (Regulation 19705.3(b)(3).) Under this
standard, unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would affect the
individual's income from or the value of the trailers by $1,000, you may participate. In
addition, if you give the trailers away and remove them from your property, you may
participate in consideration of the sea/land storage container ordinance.

QUESTION: l. Given that you have two semi-trailers for storage on your ranch, do you have a
conflict of interest in participating in the review and possfule amendment of Amador
County's sea/land storage container ordinance?
2. If you have a conflict of interest, can you sell or give away the semi-trailers,
removing them from your property, and then be free to vote?

CONCLUSION: 1. It is substantially likely that your participation in the review or amendment of
the sea/land container ordinance would affect youip".ronal finances by $250. Because
of this effect on your personal finances, you have atonflict of interest in the Board of

You must recuse yourself as discussed in this letter.
2. If you sell the semi-trailers to an individual and remove them from your
property, you may participate in the matter unless the decision on the sea/land container

intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more." Under this

participate in consideration of the sea/land storage container ordinance.
embedding.first_500_words: Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 December 26, 2007 Mr. Brian Oneto P.O. Box 95 Drytown, CA 95699 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-07-181 Dear Mr. Oneto: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS 1. Given that you have two semi-trailers for storage on your ranch, do you have a conflict of interest in participating in the review and possible amendment of Amador County's sea/land storage container ordinance? 2. If you have a conflict of interest, can you sell or give away the semi-trailers, removing them from your property, and then be free to vote? CONCLUSIONS 1. It is substantially likely that your participation in the review or amendment of the sea/land container ordinance would affect your personal finances by $250. Because of this effect on your personal finances, you have a conflict of interest in the Board of Supervisor's consideration of the sea/land container ordinance and may not participate. You must recuse yourself as discussed in this letter. 2. If you sell the semi-trailers to an individual and remove them from your property, you may participate in the matter unless the decision on the sea/land container ordinance would affect the purchaser's "income, investments, or other tangible or ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-07-181 Page No. 2 intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more." Under this standard, unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would affect the individual's income from or the value of the trailer by $1,000, you may participate. In addition, if you give the trailers away and remove them from your property, you may participate in consideration of the sea/land storage container ordinance. FACTS You are a member of the Amador County Board of Supervisors, representing District 5. In 2006, the Board approved Amador County Code 19.48.160, a copy of which you provided to us, which is designed to regulate the use and placement of sea/land and other similar storage containers. Because the ordinance was made applicable to all such containers in the unincorporated County, it contains a provision that delays enforcement until May 2008. In relevant part, Section 19.48.160(I) prohibits the use of "railroad cars, cabooses, semi-trailers, or any other unit which slides off a chassis or frame (including a body, box or unit which is removed from a chassis)" as storage containers. Amador County is looking to possibly re-write its sea-land storage container regulations. You state that many public agencies have storage containers, i.e., semi-trailers,
